question_text: China's 'Open Door Policy' and economic reforms were initiated under which Chinese leader, marking the beginning of China's transformation from a closed communist economy to a market-oriented one?
explanation: Deng Xiaoping launched China's 'Reform and Opening Up' (改革开放, Gaige Kaifang) policy from 1978, following the Third Plenary Session of the 11th Central Committee of the CPC. Deng's reforms dismantled the Maoist command economy and introduced market mechanisms — household responsibility system in agriculture, township and village enterprises, Special Economic Zones, and foreign investment. Deng famously declared 'it doesn't matter whether a cat is black or white, so long as it catches mice' — articulating pragmatic economics over ideological purity. This initiated China's extraordinary growth trajectory that transformed it from one of the world's poorest countries to the world's second-largest economy.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: Mao Zedong, during the Great Leap Forward in 1958
Deng Xiaoping, beginning in 1978 after the Third Plenary Session of the 11th CPC Central Committee
Jiang Zemin, as part of his 'Three Represents' theory in the 1990s
Xi Jinping, through the Belt and Road Initiative launched in 2013

elimination_hints.hint: The Great Leap Forward (1958-62) under Mao was a disastrous attempt at rapid industrialisation that caused a famine killing tens of millions — the opposite of a successful open-door policy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Mao's failed economic experiments with Deng's successful market reforms.

elimination_hints.hint: Jiang Zemin's 'Three Represents' (2001) extended CPC membership to capitalists but was not the initiating event of China's market reforms — that was Deng's 1978 launch.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Jiang Zemin's ideological update to market-era China with Deng's foundational launch of market reforms.

elimination_hints.hint: Xi Jinping's BRI (2013) is a recent foreign policy initiative — China's open-door and market reform began under Deng Xiaoping in 1978, 35 years before the BRI.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students associate China's global rise with Xi Jinping's BRI rather than tracing it to Deng's foundational 1978 reforms.

question_text: China's Special Economic Zones (SEZs), established from 1980 onward, were a crucial instrument of China's economic opening. What was the specific purpose and model of the SEZs?
explanation: China's first four Special Economic Zones — Shenzhen, Zhuhai, Shantou (all in Guangdong province), and Xiamen (Fujian province) — were established in 1980. SEZs were geographically delimited areas where market-oriented policies were applied before they were introduced nationwide: lower taxes for foreign investors, relaxed labour regulations, streamlined bureaucracy, and freedom to conduct business under market rules. The SEZ model allowed China to experiment with capitalism in contained zones without applying it nationwide, learning what worked before scaling up. Shenzhen, a small fishing village in 1979, became a megacity of 13+ million by 2020 — the most cited example of SEZ success.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: Geographically delimited areas with market-oriented policies — lower taxes, foreign investment welcome — to experiment with capitalism before nationwide application
Military industrial zones where China could develop weapons technology without international inspection
Agricultural communes where peasants could own land and sell surplus production at market prices
Areas where multi-party democracy was permitted to attract Western investment and political goodwill

elimination_hints.hint: SEZs were specifically civilian economic zones for manufacturing and trade — not military industrial zones; China's military development was kept entirely separate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse economic special zones with military-industrial development areas.

elimination_hints.hint: The household responsibility system (not SEZs) returned farming decision-making to peasant families; SEZs were primarily for manufacturing and foreign investment, not agricultural reform.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse SEZs (urban manufacturing/trade zones) with the rural agricultural reforms that paralleled them.

elimination_hints.hint: SEZs never involved political pluralism — the CPC maintained complete political control even in SEZs; they were economic experiments, not political ones.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine China's economic opening required political liberalisation when the entire point was separating economic from political reform.

question_text: China became a member of the World Trade Organisation (WTO) in December 2001, marking its integration into the global trading system. What was the significance of China's WTO accession for the global economy?
explanation: China's WTO accession in December 2001 was transformative for the global economy. It integrated the world's most populous country and one of its fastest-growing economies into the global trade rules system. For global trade, it meant: Chinese manufactured goods flooded global markets at prices that undercut established producers; global supply chains reorganised around Chinese manufacturing; US consumers gained access to cheaper goods (the 'China price') but US manufacturing jobs declined substantially; and China became the 'factory of the world.' China's WTO accession also committed it to transparency, intellectual property protection, and dispute resolution — though enforcement remained contested. It accelerated globalisation dramatically.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: China's WTO membership had minimal impact as China's economy was still too small to affect global trade
China's WTO accession immediately made it the world's largest economy, surpassing the USA
China's WTO integration accelerated global supply chain reorganisation around Chinese manufacturing, reshaping global trade patterns and enabling China to become the world's largest exporter
China's WTO membership required it to democratise politically as a condition of access to global trade rules

elimination_hints.hint: China had the world's 6th largest economy in 2001 and was growing at 8-10% annually — it was already a significant economic force whose WTO integration had massive global impacts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underestimate China's economic scale in 2001 which was already large enough to reshape global trade upon WTO integration.

elimination_hints.hint: China did not immediately surpass the USA at WTO accession in 2001 — it became the world's second-largest economy (by nominal GDP) around 2010, nearly a decade later.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the long-term consequence of WTO accession (eventual #2 economy status) with an immediate outcome of accession itself.

elimination_hints.hint: WTO membership has no political democratisation requirements — trade rules apply regardless of political system; China's WTO accession imposed no democratic conditions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine WTO as having political conditionality comparable to EU enlargement when WTO is purely a trade rules organisation.

question_text: China's military doctrine underwent significant transformation under President Xi Jinping from 2012 onward. Which of the following best describes the key direction of China's military modernisation?
explanation: Xi Jinping's military modernisation has pursued several key directions: (1) upgrading the PLA from a land-based army oriented toward territorial defence to a joint force capable of power projection — including blue-water naval capability, fifth-generation aircraft, hypersonic missiles, and space/cyber warfare; (2) creating the Strategic Support Force for space, cyber, and electronic warfare; (3) developing anti-access/area-denial (A2/AD) capabilities to prevent US military intervention in scenarios near China (Taiwan, South China Sea); (4) the goal of becoming a 'world-class military' by 2049 (centenary of the PRC). China now has the world's largest navy by number of ships and the largest standing army.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: China has renounced military modernisation entirely and is pursuing complete disarmament under global pressure
China's military modernisation focuses exclusively on nuclear weapons to deter US intervention
China has focused solely on land borders and has no interest in naval or air power projection
China is developing power projection capabilities including blue-water navy, fifth-generation aircraft, hypersonic missiles, and anti-access/area-denial systems

elimination_hints.hint: China has consistently increased defence spending by 6-8% annually — it is one of the world's largest military spenders, the opposite of disarmament.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse arms control rhetoric with China's substantial and sustained military build-up.

elimination_hints.hint: China's modernisation is comprehensive — conventional forces, cyber, space, and naval — not exclusively nuclear; its nuclear arsenal is actually relatively modest compared to the USA and Russia.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reduce China's broad military modernisation to its nuclear dimension alone.

elimination_hints.hint: China now has the world's largest navy by hull count and has established its first overseas military base in Djibouti — the opposite of limiting itself to land borders.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students apply Cold War-era assumptions about China's continental land power focus to a China that has decisively pivoted toward maritime power projection.

question_text: China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), announced in 2013, is described as the largest infrastructure investment programme in history. Which of the following best captures BRI's strategic dimensions beyond its stated development goals?
explanation: BRI's strategic dimensions extend well beyond infrastructure development: (1) creating economic dependencies — recipient countries that take Chinese loans for BRI projects become financially dependent on China; (2) 'debt trap diplomacy' concerns — Sri Lanka's Hambantota Port and Pakistan's CPEC have raised questions about whether unrepayable debt leads to asset acquisition by China; (3) building a China-centred economic sphere that reduces dependence on US-led financial institutions; (4) creating alternative trade routes (maritime Silk Road, overland corridors) that would allow China to bypass the US-controlled Malacca Strait and Indian Ocean; and (5) extending China's political influence in recipient countries through economic relationships. Critics note BRI also creates genuine development value — it should not be reduced entirely to malign intent.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: Creating economic dependencies, reducing reliance on US-led financial institutions, and building alternative trade routes that give China strategic options beyond US-controlled maritime chokepoints
BRI is purely humanitarian — its only goal is providing infrastructure to poor countries with no strategic or financial return
BRI is a military alliance system disguised as infrastructure investment to establish Chinese military bases globally
BRI is primarily designed to reduce China's carbon emissions through green energy infrastructure in recipient countries

elimination_hints.hint: BRI projects require commercial loans at market or near-market rates — they are not grants; China expects financial returns and strategic relationships from BRI investments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students accept China's stated development narrative without considering the financial and strategic dimensions that make BRI a geopolitical instrument.

elimination_hints.hint: China has only one confirmed overseas military base (Djibouti); BRI is primarily economic infrastructure — ports, railways, energy projects — not a military basing network.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students conflate strategic economic investment with direct military basing, overstating the military dimension of BRI.

elimination_hints.hint: While BRI does include some renewable energy projects, its dominant focus is on conventional infrastructure — coal-powered plants, ports, railways — not primarily green energy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students project contemporary climate priorities onto BRI which has historically favoured conventional infrastructure with significant environmental concerns.

question_text: The Tiananmen Square protests of 1989 and their violent suppression had profound implications for China's subsequent political trajectory. How did the CPC respond politically to prevent future similar challenges to its rule?
explanation: Following the June 4, 1989 crackdown, the CPC made several key decisions: (1) the implicit social contract — rapid economic growth in exchange for political acceptance; the party would deliver prosperity and citizens would not demand democratic rights; (2) tightening media and information control, including what became the 'Great Firewall' restricting internet access; (3) accelerating economic reforms after 1992 to demonstrate the benefits of CPC rule; (4) strengthening the party's control over the military (PLA); and (5) suppressing any organised political opposition before it could form. This 'authoritarian resilience' model — delivering economic growth while maintaining political control — became the template for China's subsequent development.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: The CPC adopted multiparty elections to legitimise its rule and prevent future protests
China abandoned economic reforms to focus exclusively on ideological education and party loyalty
The CPC deepened economic reforms to deliver prosperity as an alternative basis for legitimacy while tightening political and information control
China opened its political system to allow NGOs and civil society but maintained the CPC's ultimate authority

elimination_hints.hint: The CPC never adopted multiparty elections — the Tiananmen crackdown was specifically to prevent political liberalisation; one-party rule was firmly maintained.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume political crises lead to democratisation when in China's case the CPC specifically chose the opposite path.

elimination_hints.hint: Deng Xiaoping's 1992 'Southern Tour' specifically reinvigorated economic reform after Tiananmen — the CPC doubled down on market development as the basis for legitimacy, not pure ideology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume Tiananmen led to economic retrenchment when the CPC's response was to accelerate growth as an alternative legitimacy basis.

elimination_hints.hint: Civil society remained strictly controlled in China — NGOs operate under tight party supervision and any independent political organisation is suppressed; Tiananmen did not liberalise civil society space.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine China's post-Tiananmen response allowed expanded civil society when the crackdown specifically prevented independent civil society from developing.

question_text: China's claim to Taiwan is a central and increasingly tense issue in international politics. Which of the following correctly describes the current official positions of China, Taiwan, and the United States?
explanation: The Taiwan issue involves three distinct positions: China (PRC) claims Taiwan as an inalienable part of Chinese territory and has never renounced the use of force for reunification. Taiwan (ROC) governed by the DPP maintains a distinct identity and de facto independence, with most Taiwanese preferring the status quo to unification with the PRC. The USA maintains 'strategic ambiguity' — it acknowledges (not recognises) the Chinese position that there is one China and Taiwan is part of it, but maintains robust unofficial relations with Taiwan through the Taiwan Relations Act (1979) and sells defensive weapons to Taiwan. The USA has deliberately kept ambiguous whether it would militarily defend Taiwan — to deter both Chinese attack and Taiwanese unilateral independence declaration.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: China, Taiwan, and the USA all agree that Taiwan is an independent sovereign state

China claims Taiwan as its territory; Taiwan maintains de facto independence; the USA maintains 'strategic ambiguity' — acknowledging but not endorsing the one-China position while selling Taiwan defensive weapons
Taiwan has formally declared independence and been recognised by all major powers including the USA
The USA has signed a formal mutual defence treaty with Taiwan committing to its military defence against China

elimination_hints.hint: China explicitly claims Taiwan as part of its territory — the opposite of recognising Taiwanese independence; and the USA's position is strategic ambiguity, not recognition of independence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume Taiwan's de facto independence means international recognition of its formal sovereign statehood.

elimination_hints.hint: Taiwan has not formally declared independence — doing so would trigger a potential Chinese military response; its current status of de facto independence is deliberately left ambiguous by all parties.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students conflate Taiwan's practical self-governance with a formal independence declaration that has deliberately not been made.

elimination_hints.hint: The USA's 1979 Taiwan Relations Act replaced the earlier mutual defence treaty — the current arrangement is strategic ambiguity rather than a formal binding defence commitment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume the USA's significant arms sales to Taiwan and deployments near the Taiwan Strait constitute a formal treaty alliance equivalent to NATO.

question_text: China's rise has been described as presenting a fundamental challenge to the US-led liberal international order. Which of the following best characterises the nature of China's challenge to this order?
explanation: China's challenge to the liberal international order is complex and multidimensional: it participates in and benefits from the order (WTO, IMF, UN) while simultaneously seeking to revise certain rules it finds unfavourable. China challenges: (1) the US-led security architecture in Asia (contesting the South China Sea, pressuring Taiwan); (2) Western norms on internet governance and human rights (promoting 'internet sovereignty' against the open internet); (3) the primacy of US-led financial institutions through AIIB and NDB; (4) the dollar's reserve currency status; and (5) the norm of Western-style democracy as a universal model. China offers an alternative model — authoritarian capitalism — as proof that development without democracy is viable. It is a 'selective revisionist' — not seeking to overthrow the order but to reshape it in China's favour.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: China seeks to completely destroy the international order and replace it with Chinese global governance
China fully accepts the liberal international order and is a status quo power with no revisionist ambitions
China is a 'selective revisionist' — participating in the existing order while building alternative institutions and contesting specific rules it finds unfavourable
China's challenge is purely economic — it accepts all political and security rules while competing economically

elimination_hints.hint: China benefits enormously from the existing order — WTO membership, IMF voting rights, UN Security Council veto — and has no interest in overthrowing an order that benefits it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume China's rise necessarily involves revolutionary overthrow of the entire international order rather than selective reshaping.

elimination_hints.hint: China's South China Sea island-building, rejection of UNCLOS arbitration, and human rights norms challenges show it is not fully accepting all liberal order rules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students accept China's 'peaceful rise' rhetoric as meaning full acceptance of the liberal order when its behaviour clearly challenges specific rules.

elimination_hints.hint: China's challenges are decidedly not only economic — its South China Sea military assertiveness, Taiwan policy, and human rights norm challenges all have major political and security dimensions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students compartmentalise China's challenge as purely economic when its security and normative dimensions are equally or more significant.

question_text: The Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO), which China co-founded in 2001, has expanded to include India and Pakistan (2017) and Iran (2023). Which of the following best describes the SCO's purpose and China's role within it?
explanation: The SCO was founded in June 2001 by China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan — building on the earlier 'Shanghai Five' security dialogue. Its founding purposes were: combating the 'three evils' (terrorism, separatism, extremism), drug trafficking, and border security cooperation. China uses the SCO to: project influence into Central Asia; coordinate with Russia on security matters; frame counter-terrorism in ways that justify domestic crackdowns on Uyghurs and other minorities; and provide an institutional alternative to US-led organisations. With India's 2017 accession, the SCO covers 40% of the world's population. India's membership creates interesting tensions given India-China and India-Pakistan border conflicts within the same organisation.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: A China-Russia-led security and political organisation covering Central Asia that frames counter-terrorism cooperation and serves as an institutional alternative to Western-led bodies
A free trade agreement among Central Asian nations led by China and Russia to reduce tariffs
A nuclear weapons coordination forum for non-NPT states seeking to jointly develop nuclear capability
A UN-sponsored peacekeeping force operating in Central Asia under Chinese command

elimination_hints.hint: The SCO is a security and political organisation — trade integration is primarily handled through bilateral FTAs and RCEP/EAEU, not the SCO.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the SCO's security-political mandate with economic integration frameworks.

elimination_hints.hint: SCO members include NPT signatories (Russia, China, Kazakhstan) and non-NPT nuclear states (India, Pakistan) — it is not a nuclear weapons coordination forum.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invent a nuclear coordination dimension to the SCO which has no such mandate.

elimination_hints.hint: The SCO is a sovereign state-led organisation, not a UN body; it operates independently and is often seen as an alternative to rather than extension of Western-dominated multilateral institutions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students conflate the SCO with UN-mandated frameworks when it is precisely designed as an alternative to Western-led multilateralism.

question_text: The 'Thucydides Trap' concept, applied to China's rise by Harvard scholar Graham Allison, makes a specific historical argument about the dangers of a rising power. What does this concept predict about US-China relations?
explanation: Graham Allison's 'Thucydides Trap' (from his 2017 book 'Destined for War?') draws on Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War — which began because 'the growth of Athenian power and the fear this caused in Sparta made war inevitable.' Allison examined 16 historical cases of a rising power threatening a ruling power and found that 12 of 16 resulted in war. Applied to the USA-China relationship, the thesis warns that as China's power grows toward or potentially surpassing American power, structural pressures toward conflict intensify — even if neither side wants war. Allison does not claim war is inevitable but argues it is more likely than most assume and requires deliberate, extraordinary diplomacy to avoid.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: China and the USA will inevitably cooperate because both are nuclear powers and mutual destruction makes war impossible
China will peacefully overtake the USA economically without any military conflict due to economic interdependence
The USA will preemptively attack China before it becomes too powerful to defeat — as Athens struck Sparta

As China's power approaches US levels, structural pressures toward conflict intensify — historically 12 of 16 such transitions resulted in war — making extraordinary diplomacy necessary to avoid conflict

elimination_hints.hint: Allison explicitly argues that nuclear deterrence does not eliminate the Thucydides Trap dynamic — nuclear powers have still come close to war (Cuba 1962) and the structural pressures remain.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume nuclear deterrence solves the Thucydides Trap when Allison specifically argues otherwise.

elimination_hints.hint: Allison examines historical cases and finds economic interdependence did not prevent war (e.g., Germany and Britain were major trading partners before World War I).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students apply liberal interdependence theory (trade prevents war) when Allison specifically rejects this as sufficient protection against the Thucydides Trap.

elimination_hints.hint: Allison's thesis is about structural pressures toward conflict from the rising power's perspective, not a prescription for preemptive US attack; Athens in his Sparta analogy was the established power, not the rising one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reverse the Thucydides dynamic — in the US-China context, China is the rising power (Athens role) and the USA is the established power (Sparta role).

question_text:
Match the following milestones or features of China's rise in Column A with their correct descriptions or years in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) China's WTO accession | (P) 2013 — Xi Jinping's signature foreign policy initiative for infrastructure investment globally |
| (ii) 'One Country, Two Systems' | (Q) December 2001 — integrated China into global trading rules, transforming supply chains |
| (iii) Belt and Road Initiative | (R) Framework under which Hong Kong and Macau were returned to China while retaining distinct legal systems |
| (iv) Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB) | (S) 2015 — China-led multilateral development bank as alternative to World Bank/IMF |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: 2013 — Xi Jinping's signature foreign policy initiative for infrastructure investment globally
December 2001 — integrated China into global trading rules, transforming supply chains
Framework under which Hong Kong and Macau were returned to China while retaining distinct legal systems
2015 — China-led multilateral development bank as alternative to World Bank/IMF

explanation: China's WTO accession was December 2001 (Q). One Country Two Systems was the framework for Hong Kong (1997) and Macau (1999) returns (R). BRI was launched by Xi Jinping in 2013 (P). AIIB was established in 2015 as a China-led development bank (S).
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: i-P, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-Q, ii-S, iii-P, iv-R
i-R, ii-Q, iii-P, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes BRI (2013 Xi initiative), not WTO accession; WTO accession was December 2001 (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse WTO accession (global trade integration) with BRI (China-led infrastructure initiative).

elimination_hints.hint: S describes AIIB (2015 development bank), not One Country Two Systems; One Country Two Systems is the Hong Kong/Macau governance framework (R).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the governance framework for Hong Kong/Macau (One Country Two Systems) with the development bank (AIIB).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes One Country Two Systems (Hong Kong/Macau), not WTO accession; WTO accession was trade integration (Q), not a territorial governance arrangement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse WTO trade integration (Q) with One Country Two Systems territorial governance (R).

question_text:
Match the following aspects of China-India relations in Column A with their correct descriptions or events in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) 1962 Sino-Indian War | (P) India's October 1962 military defeat that shattered Nehru's 'Hindi-Chini Bhai Bhai' vision |
| (ii) Panchsheel Agreement (1954) | (Q) Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence — basis of early India-China diplomatic relations |
| (iii) China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) | (R) India's primary objection to BRI — passes through Pakistan-Administered Kashmir |
| (iv) Doklam/Galwan standoffs | (S) 2017 and 2020 military confrontations on contested India-China border |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: India's October 1962 military defeat that shattered Nehru's 'Hindi-Chini Bhai Bhai' vision
Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence — basis of early India-China diplomatic relations
India's primary objection to BRI — passes through Pakistan-Administered Kashmir
2017 and 2020 military confrontations on contested India-China border

explanation: The 1962 war ended in India's defeat, shattering Nehru's vision (P). Panchsheel (1954) articulated Five Principles including peaceful coexistence (Q). CPEC passes through Pakistan-Administered Kashmir making India refuse to join BRI (R). Doklam (2017) and Galwan (2020) were the major recent India-China border confrontations (S).
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-P, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-S
i-S, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-P

elimination_hints.hint: Q describes Panchsheel (Five Principles), not the 1962 war; the 1962 war was the military defeat (P) that shattered Panchsheel's 'peaceful coexistence' framework.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Panchsheel's peaceful coexistence principles with the 1962 war that violated them.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes CPEC/Kashmir (India's BRI objection), not Panchsheel; Panchsheel articulated Five Principles (Q), while CPEC represents India's current BRI objection (R).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Panchsheel's foundational diplomatic principles with India's contemporary CPEC objection.

elimination_hints.hint: S describes Doklam/Galwan (2017/2020 confrontations), not the 1962 war; the 1962 war was a full-scale conflict (P), distinct from the more recent standoffs (S).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the 1962 full-scale war with the more recent Doklam and Galwan border standoffs.

question_text:
Match the following analytical frameworks or concepts about China's rise in Column A with their correct authors or descriptions in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) 'Peaceful Rise' / 'Peaceful Development' | (P) John Mearsheimer — China cannot rise peacefully because great power competition is inevitable |
| (ii) 'China Threat Theory' | (Q) China's own narrative — economic development not requiring confrontation with existing powers |
| (iii): 'Offensive Realism' applied to China | (R) Scholars arguing China's growing military/economic power will inevitably create conflict with the USA |
| (iv) 'Authoritarian Capitalism model' | (S) China demonstrating that rapid development is achievable without liberal democracy — challenging Western development orthodoxy |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: John Mearsheimer — China cannot rise peacefully because great power competition is inevitable
China's own narrative — economic development not requiring confrontation with existing powers
Scholars arguing China's growing military/economic power will inevitably create conflict with the USA
China demonstrating that rapid development is achievable without liberal democracy — challenging Western development orthodoxy

explanation: Peaceful Rise/Development is China's own narrative about non-confrontational ascent (Q). China Threat Theory is the Western/US-centric view of inevitable conflict from China's growth (R). Offensive Realism applied to China is specifically Mearsheimer's structural argument that China cannot rise peacefully (P). Authoritarian Capitalism describes China's model as an alternative to the liberal development consensus (S).
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: i-R, ii-Q, iii-P, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-P, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: R describes China Threat Theory (Western concern about inevitable conflict), not Peaceful Rise; Peaceful Rise is China's own reassuring narrative (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse China's self-narrative (peaceful rise) with external threat-based assessments (China threat theory).

elimination_hints.hint: P describes Mearsheimer's offensive realism (specific structural inevitability argument), not China Threat Theory in general; China Threat Theory is the broader category (R), while Mearsheimer offers a specific theoretical version (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the general China threat discourse (R) with Mearsheimer's specific offensive realism application (P).

elimination_hints.hint: P describes Mearsheimer's offensive realism, not Peaceful Rise; Peaceful Rise is China's self-narrative (Q), not a Western academic structural theory.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse China's diplomatic narrative (Peaceful Rise) with Mearsheimer's academic structural argument (Offensive Realism).

assertion: China's rise represents the most significant challenge to US hegemony not because China will militarily defeat the USA but because it offers an alternative model of development that delegitimises the liberal democratic template.
reason: China's sustained economic growth under single-party authoritarian governance demonstrates to developing countries that rapid modernisation does not require Western-style democracy, undermining the US narrative that economic development and political liberalisation are inseparable.
question_text:
Assertion (A): China's rise represents the most significant challenge to US hegemony not because China will militarily defeat the USA but because it offers an alternative model of development that delegitimises the liberal democratic template.
Reason (R): China's sustained economic growth under single-party authoritarian governance demonstrates to developing countries that rapid modernisation does not require Western-style democracy, undermining the US narrative that economic development and political liberalisation are inseparable.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. China's authoritarian capitalism has attracted significant attention from developing country governments seeking to grow without adopting Western political conditions. R correctly identifies the mechanism — empirical demonstration that growth ≠ democracy — that challenges the ideological foundation of US-led liberal hegemony in A. This normative/model challenge may be more durable than any military challenge.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies the specific mechanism — empirical demonstration that growth is possible without democracy — that directly explains why China's model challenges US ideological hegemony as described in A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's strategic claim and R's empirical evidence as parallel rather than explanation-explanandum.

elimination_hints.hint: R is empirically grounded — China's 40-year growth record under authoritarian governance is the most cited counterexample to the democratisation-development nexus that US development doctrine promotes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question whether China's growth under authoritarian governance constitutes a genuine challenge to liberal development orthodoxy.

elimination_hints.hint: A is analytically defensible — China's model has inspired 'authoritarian capitalist' experiments in countries like Rwanda, Ethiopia, and others, showing genuine appeal as an alternative to Western conditionality-laden development assistance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students dismiss the ideological/model challenge as insignificant compared to military competition when it may be more fundamental to hegemonic sustainability.

assertion: The 'China Threat' narrative promoted by the United States is partly a strategic construction designed to maintain US hegemony by framing China as a danger requiring a US-led response, rather than an objective assessment of Chinese intentions.
reason: While China does present real strategic challenges, the threat narrative often exaggerates Chinese capabilities and intentions, obscures the role of US behaviour (military presence near China, Taiwan arms sales) in provoking Chinese responses, and serves the purpose of justifying US military spending and alliance maintenance.
question_text:
Assertion (A): The 'China Threat' narrative promoted by the United States is partly a strategic construction designed to maintain US hegemony by framing China as a danger requiring a US-led response, rather than an objective assessment of Chinese intentions.
Reason (R): While China does present real strategic challenges, the threat narrative often exaggerates Chinese capabilities and intentions, obscures the role of US behaviour (military presence near China, Taiwan arms sales) in provoking Chinese responses, and serves the purpose of justifying US military spending and alliance maintenance.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically defensible. Critical IR theory and China Studies scholars have pointed out that 'threat construction' is a real political process — states frame rivals as threats to justify their own policies. R accurately notes that the narrative can exaggerate and obscures US provocations. However, this requires care: real Chinese military expansion, SCS island-building, and Taiwan threats are documented — the statement is not that China poses no challenge, but that the narrative is partly constructed for US domestic political purposes. A and R present a critical perspective that is analytically defensible even if contested.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R's specific points — narrative exaggeration, obscured US provocations, political justification purposes — directly constitute the 'strategic construction' mechanism described in A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's critical framing and R's supporting evidence as parallel rather than as explanation-explanandum.

elimination_hints.hint: R is analytically defensible — documented examples of threat exaggeration exist (e.g., 'China gap' debates), US naval presence near China does create security dilemma dynamics, and military-industrial interests do benefit from threat inflation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reject R entirely, assuming all US threat assessments of China are fully accurate with no strategic construction component.

elimination_hints.hint: A is a defensible analytical position in critical security studies — 'securitisation theory' (Buzan, Waever) specifically analyses how threats are socially constructed for political purposes, a framework widely applied to US-China discourse.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume A is false because it challenges an official US narrative, when it represents a legitimate and documented scholarly perspective on how threat narratives function.

assertion: China's economic growth has created as many internal vulnerabilities as external strengths, suggesting that the 'China overtaking America' narrative may be oversimplified.
reason: China faces severe internal challenges — a rapidly ageing population, massive debt in real estate and local governments, growing inequality between coastal and interior regions, environmental degradation, and the 'middle income trap' risk — that could constrain its continued rise.
question_text:
Assertion (A): China's economic growth has created as many internal vulnerabilities as external strengths, suggesting that the 'China overtaking America' narrative may be oversimplified.
Reason (R): China faces severe internal challenges — a rapidly ageing population, massive debt in real estate and local governments, growing inequality between coastal and interior regions, environmental degradation, and the 'middle income trap' risk — that could constrain its continued rise.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. China's remarkable growth has indeed generated structural vulnerabilities: its population ages faster than it grew rich; total debt exceeds 300% of GDP; the Evergrande property crisis revealed systemic real estate risks; income per capita remains about $12,000 vs USA's $70,000+; and environmental costs are enormous. R provides the specific internal challenges that directly explain why A's caution about the 'China overtaking America' narrative is analytically justified.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R's specific vulnerabilities — ageing population, debt, inequality, environmental damage, middle income trap — are precisely the internal challenges that explain A's caution about the rise narrative.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's interpretive caution and R's factual vulnerabilities as parallel rather than as the evidence base for A's qualified assessment.

elimination_hints.hint: R is empirically documented — China's population is ageing rapidly (one-child policy legacy), Evergrande and property sector debt are real systemic risks, coastal-interior inequality is extensive, and China's per capita income remains far below the USA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students dismiss R's structural vulnerabilities, perhaps assuming China's GDP trajectory automatically means all these challenges will be overcome.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by economists from Nicholas Lardy to Michael Pettis who argue China's structural vulnerabilities could derail its rise — the 'China overtaking America by 2030' predictions have repeatedly been revised.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students accept the China rise narrative uncritically, dismissing A's qualified assessment that internal vulnerabilities complicate the overtaking story.

question_text:
Arrange the following milestones in China's rise as a global power in the correct chronological sequence:

(i) China becomes the world's second-largest economy by nominal GDP, surpassing Japan
(ii) Deng Xiaoping launches 'Reform and Opening Up' — special economic zones, agricultural reform
(iii) China hosts the Beijing Olympics — projecting soft power and demonstrating its global arrival
(iv) China launches the Belt and Road Initiative under Xi Jinping

items.text: China becomes the world's second-largest economy by nominal GDP, surpassing Japan
Deng Xiaoping launches 'Reform and Opening Up' — special economic zones, agricultural reform
China hosts the Beijing Olympics — projecting soft power and demonstrating its global arrival
China launches the Belt and Road Initiative under Xi Jinping

explanation: Deng's Reform and Opening Up began from 1978 (ii). Beijing Olympics were held in August 2008 (iii). China surpassed Japan to become the world's 2nd largest nominal GDP economy in 2010 (i). BRI was launched in 2013 (iv). This sequence traces China's rise from domestic reform through global event to economic milestone to global infrastructure ambition.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: iii → ii → i → iv
ii → iii → i → iv
ii → i → iii → iv
ii → iii → iv → i

elimination_hints.hint: The 2008 Olympics (iii) could not precede Deng's 1978 reforms (ii) — the reforms are the foundational event that enabled China's subsequent achievements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the Beijing Olympics before the reforms that created the economic foundation for hosting them.

elimination_hints.hint: China surpassed Japan in 2010 (i) after the 2008 Olympics (iii) — placing i before iii reverses this order.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place China's GDP surpassing Japan before the 2008 Olympics when it happened two years after the Olympics.

elimination_hints.hint: China surpassed Japan (i, 2010) before launching BRI (iv, 2013) — placing iv before i reverses these events.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the BRI before China's GDP milestone when BRI came after China had already consolidated its second-largest-economy status.

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the correct logical sequence showing how China's growing power has progressively challenged US hegemony in the Asia-Pacific:

(i) China begins large-scale construction of artificial islands in the South China Sea, installing military infrastructure
(ii) China joins WTO and deepens economic integration with USA and global supply chains
(iii) US announces 'Pivot to Asia' — increasing military presence and ASEAN engagement to counter China's rise
(iv) China launches AIIB and BRI as alternative institutional frameworks to Western-led development architecture

items.text: China begins large-scale construction of artificial islands in the South China Sea, installing military infrastructure
China joins WTO and deepens economic integration with USA and global supply chains
US announces 'Pivot to Asia' — increasing military presence and ASEAN engagement to counter China's rise
China launches AIIB and BRI as alternative institutional frameworks to Western-led development architecture

explanation: China joined WTO in December 2001 (ii). The US Pivot to Asia was announced in November 2011 (iii). BRI was announced September 2013 and AIIB was founded 2015 (iv). Large-scale SCS island construction accelerated from 2013-2016, with military infrastructure building from 2014-2016 (i). This shows economic integration → US response → China's institutional alternatives → direct territorial assertion.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: ii → iii → iv → i
i → ii → iii → iv
ii → i → iii → iv
iii → ii → iv → i

elimination_hints.hint: SCS island construction (i, 2013-2016) cannot precede WTO accession (ii, 2001) — China had to build economic strength through trade integration before it could assert itself territorially.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place China's assertive territorial action before the economic integration that preceded and enabled China's subsequent confidence.

elimination_hints.hint: SCS island construction (i, 2013-2016) came after the US Pivot (iii, 2011) — China's assertive island-building was partly a response to the pivot.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place SCS island-building before the US Pivot announcement that preceded and partly triggered it.

elimination_hints.hint: China's WTO accession (ii, 2001) preceded the US Pivot (iii, 2011) by a decade — the Pivot was specifically a response to China's growing power post-WTO, not something that preceded WTO integration.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the US response (Pivot) before the Chinese development (WTO integration) that necessitated the response.

question_text: China's economic development since 1978 has been universally praised as a model for developing countries to follow, with no significant costs or downsides to China's population or the global environment.
explanation: This statement is false. China's economic rise has been extraordinary but came with severe costs: the 2008 melamine milk scandal, 2010s smog crises in Beijing and other cities representing some of the world's worst air pollution; a Gini coefficient that rose to among the highest in the world, with coastal-interior and urban-rural inequality growing dramatically; forced displacement of hundreds of millions for infrastructure projects; suppression of labour rights and independent unions; massive environmental damage including water pollution, soil contamination, and destruction of biodiversity; and the social costs of rapid urbanisation. While poverty reduction has been spectacular (800 million+ lifted out of poverty), characterising China's development as 'universally praised with no costs' ignores these significant negative dimensions documented extensively by Chinese academics, civil society, and international observers.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: The statement contains a factual error. This statement is false. The option 'True' incorrectly accepts the claim.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students may accept the plausible-sounding statement without recalling the precise facts.

question_text:
Study the following table showing China's key power indicators at different points in its rise:

| Indicator | 1978 | 1990 | 2000 | 2010 | 2022 |
|-----------|------|------|------|------|------|
| GDP (nominal, USD trillion) | 0.15 | 0.36 | 1.21 | 6.0 | 18.0 |
| GDP per capita (USD) | ~160 | ~340 | ~960 | ~4,500 | ~12,500 |
| Share of world manufacturing | ~3% | ~4% | ~9% | ~19% | ~29% |
| Defence spending (USD billion) | ~30 | ~12 | ~42 | ~119 | ~293 |
| Number of overseas military bases | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 (Djibouti) |
| BRI countries involved | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | 150+ |
| WTO membership | No | No | No | Yes (since 2001) | Yes |
| UN Security Council P5 member | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is MOST accurately supported about the nature of China's rise?
explanation: The table reveals several important patterns: China's rise is overwhelmingly economic (29% of world manufacturing, GDP from $0.15T to $18T) while its military expansion is more constrained (only one overseas base vs USA's 700+). GDP per capita remains at ~$12,500 — far below the USA's ~$75,000 — indicating China is still a middle-income country despite large aggregate GDP. The data shows a clear escalating trajectory but also the gap between aggregate and per-capita wealth. China has been a P5 member throughout — it leveraged institutional position from the beginning. This supports the conclusion that China's rise is primarily economic in character with military modernisation lagging significantly behind economic growth, and that per capita development still has far to go.
topic: Rise of China as a Global Power
subject: political-science

options.text: China has already surpassed the USA as the world's dominant military power, as shown by its defence spending trajectory.
China's GDP per capita of $12,500 in 2022 means it is already a wealthy developed nation fully comparable to the USA.
China's rise has been primarily military in character, with economic growth being a secondary consequence of military expansion.

China's rise is overwhelmingly economic in its foundation (29% of world manufacturing, $18T GDP) while its military projection capability remains limited (one overseas base), and its per capita income still has a large gap from developed-world levels.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows China has one overseas military base vs the USA's 700+; defence spending at $293B vs USA's ~$800B — China has significant military but has not surpassed US military dominance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students conflate China's military modernisation trajectory with having already achieved US-level military dominance.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows China's per capita GDP at ~$12,500 in 2022 vs the USA's ~$75,000 — a 6:1 ratio; $12,500 places China in the upper-middle income category, not 'fully comparable to the USA'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse China's aggregate GDP size with per capita wealth levels, conflating the two very different measures.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows China's manufacturing share growing from 3% to 29% and GDP from $0.15T to $18T — overwhelmingly economic dimensions; the military data (one base, $293B spending) is secondary to the economic trajectory.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invert China's actual development trajectory, which was economic-led with military modernisation following economic success.
